A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Psychology becomes a science · 19th c.

Nineteenth Century

In the long nineteenth century the philosophy of mind splits and recombines as psychology, logic, physiology, evolution, and idealism all emerge as distinct but entangled disciplines. The period becomes a laboratory for associationism, psychophysics, comparative psychology, German idealism, and the first truly scientific psychologies.

1773 – 1836

James Mill

Associationist psychology as a mechanics of the mind.

1776 – 1841

Johann Friedrich Herbart

Toward a mathematical psychology: the statics and dynamics of mental representations.

1778 – 1820

Thomas Brown

A Scottish philosopher-physician on perception, association, and the reform of common sense.

1781 – 1848

Bernard Bolzano

Propositions in themselves and the ideal contents of judgment — before phenomenology, before logic as we know it.

1788 – 1856

William Hamilton

A Scottish Kantian on knowledge, feeling, conation — and the philosophy of the conditioned.

1806 – 1873

John Stuart Mill

Mental chemistry, the permanent possibility of sensation, and other minds as inference.

1817 – 1881

Hermann Lotze

A bridge from idealism to empirical psychology: soul, value, and the unity of mental life.

1818 – 1903

Alexander Bain

The psycho-physiologist behind British associationism and the founder of Mind.

1832 – 1912

Shadworth Hodgson

A patient metaphysician of experience who helped name the philosophy of mind.

1837 – 1912

Victoria Welby

The founder of significs: meaning, translation, and the expressive life of mind.

1838 – 1916

Ernst Mach

Neutral monism, the analysis of sensations, and the self as a “bundle of elements.”

1839 – 1914

Charles Sanders Peirce

Pragmatism, semiotics, habit, and a mind whose structure is continuous with the cosmos.

1848 – 1922

E. E. Constance Jones

A Cambridge logician on attention, dualism, and the logic of character.

1848 – 1925

Gottlob Frege

Thoughts are not ideas: the anti-psychologist who redrew the boundary between mind and logic.

1842 – 1892

George Croom Robertson

The first editor of Mind: philosophy of mind as an emerging scientific discipline.

1847 – 1930

Christine Ladd-Franklin

A phenomenal primitivist and experimental psychophysicist on consciousness and color.

1859 – 1941

Henri Bergson

Bergson’s philosophy of mind can be extracted as a temporal, action-oriented spiritual realism.

1809 – 1882

Charles Darwin

Evolution, expression, and continuity between animal and human minds.

1801 – 1887

Gustav Fechner

Psychophysics, panpsychism, and the ensouled continuity of nature.

1762 – 1814

J. G. Fichte

Self-positing activity and mind as world-making freedom.

1770 – 1831

G. W. F. Hegel

Geist, recognition, and the historical unfolding of minded life.

1839 – 1916

Théodule Ribot

What follows is a reconstruction rather than a simple summary.

1848 – 1894

George Romanes

George John Romanes should not be read as a thinker with one frozen doctrine.

1775 – 1854

F. W. J. Schelling

Nature, freedom, and spirit as the self-unfolding of the absolute.

1788 – 1860

Arthur Schopenhauer

Will, representation, and the suffering subject at the root of world.

1834 – 1900

Carl Georg Lange

Emotion, physiology, and the bodily constitution of felt life.

1852 – 1936

C. Lloyd Morgan

Emergence, animal mind, and graded continuity between life and consciousness.

1835 – 1902

Samuel Butler

Memory, habit, and the evolutionary continuity of mind and life.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
